flashcards.front: What is Process Scheduling in operating systems?
flashcards.back: Process scheduling is the mechanism by which an operating system decides which process should occupy the CPU at any given time, managing the allocation of CPU resources among multiple competing processes.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of a Long-term Scheduler?
flashcards.back: The long-term scheduler (admission scheduler) decides which processes are admitted to the ready queue from the new state. It controls the degree of multiprogramming and balances system throughput.

flashcards.front: What is the role of a Short-term Scheduler?
flashcards.back: The short-term scheduler (CPU scheduler) selects which process from the ready queue should get the CPU next. It is invoked very frequently (milliseconds) and must be fast.

flashcards.front: Define CPU Utilization as a scheduling criterion.
flashcards.back: CPU Utilization measures the percentage of time the CPU is busy doing useful work. In real systems, it ranges from 40% (light load) to 90% (heavy load). Higher utilization means better system efficiency.

flashcards.front: What is Throughput in process scheduling?
flashcards.back: Throughput is the number of processes that complete their execution per unit time. Higher throughput indicates the system is completing more work efficiently.
Turnaround Time = Completion Time - Arrival Time. It represents the total time from when a process arrives in the ready queue until it completes execution.

flashcards.front: What is Waiting Time in CPU scheduling?
flashcards.back: Waiting Time = Turnaround Time - Burst Time. It is the total time a process spends waiting in the ready queue for CPU allocation.
Response Time = Time when process first gets CPU - Arrival Time. For interactive systems, minimizing response time is crucial for user satisfaction.
Preemptive scheduling allows the OS to forcibly remove the CPU from a running process and assign it to another process. This enables handling time-critical events but adds overhead.
Non-preemptive scheduling once the CPU is assigned to a process, it keeps the CPU until it terminates or switches to waiting state. It is simpler but may cause longer waiting for other processes.
The medium-term scheduler temporarily removes processes from memory (swaps them out) to reduce degree of multiprogramming during heavy load, improving system responsiveness.

A context switch is the process of saving the state of one process and restoring the state of another when switching the CPU between processes. It involves storing registers, program counter, and other CPU states.

flashcards.front: What is Dispatcher in CPU scheduling?
flashcards.back: The dispatcher is the component that gives control of the CPU to the process selected by the short-term scheduler. It involves context switching and switching to user mode.
The ready queue is a data structure containing all processes that are in the ready state, waiting for CPU allocation. It may be implemented as a queue, linked list, or priority queue.
Degree of multiprogramming refers to the number of processes currently in the ready state or running state. The long-term scheduler controls this to optimize CPU utilization and system performance.
